From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Quota quickies](/w/index.php?title=Quota_quickies&redirect=no "Quota quickies"))

Act to stimulate the British film industry

Cinematograph Films Act 1927

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Act of Parliament | |
| [coat of arms](/wiki/File:Coat_of_arms_of_the_United_Kingdom_(1901%E2%80%931952).svg) [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") | |
| [Long title](/wiki/Short_and_long_titles "Short and long titles") | An Act to restrict blind booking and advance booking of cinematograph films, and to secure the renting and exhibition of a certain proportion of British films, and for purposes connected therewith. |
| [Citation](/wiki/Citation_of_United_Kingdom_legislation "Citation of United Kingdom legislation") | [17 & 18 Geo. 5](/wiki/17_%26_18_Geo._5 "17 & 18 Geo. 5"). c. 29 |
| Dates | |
| [Royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent") | 22 December 1927 |
| [Commencement](/wiki/Coming_into_force "Coming into force") | 1 April 1928 |
| Other legislation | |
| Amended by | Cinematograph Films Act 1938 |
| [Repealed by](/wiki/Repeal "Repeal") | [Films Act 1960](/w/index.php?title=Films_Act_1960&action=edit&redlink=1 "Films Act 1960 (page does not exist)") |
| Status: Repealed | |

The **Cinematograph Films Act 1927** ([17 & 18 Geo. 5](/wiki/17_%26_18_Geo._5 "17 & 18 Geo. 5"). c. 29) was an [act](/wiki/Act_of_parliament "Act of parliament") of the [UK Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") designed to stimulate the declining [British film industry](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_Kingdom "Cinema of the United Kingdom"). It received [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent") on 22 December 1927[[1]](#cite_note-1) and came into force on 1 April 1928.

## Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cinematograph_Films_Act_1927&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Description")]

The act introduced a requirement for British cinemas to show a quota of British films for a duration of 10 years. Its supporters believed that it would promote the emergence of a vertically integrated film industry, with production, distribution and exhibition infrastructure controlled by the same companies. As the [vertically integrated American film industry](/wiki/Studio_system "Studio system") had rapid growth in the years immediately following the end of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), the intention was to counter Hollywood's perceived economic and cultural dominance by promoting similar business practices among British studios, distributors and cinema chains.

By creating an obligatory market-section for British films, it was hoped that the increased economic activity in the production sector would lead to the growth of a self-sustaining industry. The quota was initially set at 7.5% for exhibitors but was raised to 20% in 1935. The films included those shot in British dominions such as Canada and Australia.

A British film was defined according to the following criteria:

* The film must be made by a British or British-controlled company.
* Studio scenes must be photographed within a film studio in the British Empire.
* The author of the scenario or the original work on which the screenplay was based must be a British subject.
* At least 75% of the salaries must be paid to British subjects,[[2]](#cite_note-2) excluding the costs of two persons, at least one of whom must be an actor. (That provision allowed for a British film to include a highly paid non-British star, producer, or director but still be regarded as a British film.)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Hollywood studios had to comply with the quota if they wanted their productions shown in Britain. Rather than forfeiting the lucrative British market, the studios contracted with British producers to make low-budget features for them, so their American productions could be distributed alongside them in Britain. These cheap, homegrown features became known as [quota quickies](/wiki/Category:Quota_quickies "Category:Quota quickies").

## Consequences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cinematograph_Films_Act_1927&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Consequences")]

The act is generally considered unsuccessful. It fostered speculative investment in lavishly budgeted features for which production costs could not have been recouped on the domestic market, and it was criticised for the emergence of the inferior "quota quickies".

| Cinematograph Films Act 1938 | |
| --- | --- |
| Act of Parliament | |
| [coat of arms](/wiki/File:Coat_of_arms_of_the_United_Kingdom_(1901%E2%80%931952).svg) [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") | |
| [Long title](/wiki/Short_and_long_titles "Short and long titles") | An Act to make further provision for securing the renting and exhibition of a certain proportion of British cinematograph films, and for restricting blind booking and advance booking of cinematograph films; to make provision as to the wages and conditions of employment of persons employed by makers of cinematograph films; and to provide for purposes connected with the matters aforesaid. |
| [Citation](/wiki/Citation_of_United_Kingdom_legislation "Citation of United Kingdom legislation") | [1 & 2 Geo. 6](/wiki/1_%26_2_Geo._6 "1 & 2 Geo. 6"). c. 17 |
| Dates | |
| [Royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent") | 30 March 1938 |
| [Commencement](/wiki/Coming_into_force "Coming into force") | 1 April 1938 |
| Other legislation | |
| Amends | Cinematograph Films Act 1927 |
| [Text of statute as originally enacted](https://www.legislation.gov.uk/ukpga/1938/17/pdfs/ukpga_19380017_en.pdf) | |

The act was modified by the **Cinematograph Films Act 1938** ([1 & 2 Geo. 6](/wiki/1_%26_2_Geo._6 "1 & 2 Geo. 6"). c. 17), which removed films shot in British Empire countries from the quota. It was, in turn, amended by further acts, and it was eventually repealed by the [Films Act 1960](/w/index.php?title=Films_Act_1960&action=edit&redlink=1 "Films Act 1960 (page does not exist)") ([8 & 9 Eliz. 2](/wiki/8_%26_9_Eliz._2 "8 & 9 Eliz. 2"). c. 57).

In recent years, an alternative view has arisen among film historians, such as Lawrence Napper, who have argued that the quota quickie has been too casually dismissed, and is of particular cultural and historical value because such films often contained performances unique to British popular culture, such as [music hall](/wiki/Music_hall "Music hall") and [variety acts](/wiki/Variety_show "Variety show"), that would not have been filmed under normal circumstances.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
